You are a CLEAN-ROOM implementer. Your work will be legally relied upon as an
independent creation, and your complete tool-call transcript is retained as evidence. STRICT RULES:
1. Work ONLY inside your cluster directory. First action: read PROTOCOL.md at
   /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md, then your SPEC.md and interface/ files.
2. NEVER read any file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ (source, git, anything). The ONLY
   exceptions: EXECUTING the oracle scripts in your oracle/ dir, and READING files under your
   oracle/captured_responses/ or oracle/captures/ or oracle/examples/ (captured program output
   and observation inputs). Do not use git anywhere. Do not fetch tamarin-prover source or
   internals documentation from the web.
3. Do NOT reproduce tamarin-prover implementation internals from your training memory. Every
   behavior claim must trace to an oracle observation, a capture, or the SPEC. Exact output
   strings must be copied from OBSERVED oracle output (compatibility content). If you notice
   yourself recalling how tamarin-prover's source does something, discard that and observe instead.
4. Log every oracle interaction's purpose in workspace/QUERIES.log. Maintain workspace/BEHAVIOR.md
   as you learn. Design your own internal structure — do not try to guess the original's.
Your final structured report summarizes components, tests, coverage, and gaps.

Your cluster directory: /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/wellformedness/
Task per SPEC.md: build the wf-clean crate in workspace/ implementing the wellformedness
checker from oracle behavior. Aim for COMPLETE coverage of every check the oracle performs:
enumerate checks by mutating the example inputs and minimal hand-written theories until new
warning topics stop appearing (also mine oracle/captures/ for topics you haven't triggered).
Implement the API in interface/required_api.md over the interface/ast_types.rs model, with
tests per check, cargo test passing. Work as long as needed; report gaps honestly.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).
47

1	# Clean-room task: wellformedness checker
2	
3	Build a standalone Rust crate `wf-clean` in workspace/ that reimplements,
4	from BLACK-BOX BEHAVIOR ONLY, the wellformedness checking of the
5	tamarin-prover binary (the oracle).
6	
7	## What the oracle does
8	`oracle/wf_oracle.sh <file.spthy>` prints the tool's full output for a
9	security-protocol theory file. Within that output, wellformedness checking
10	produces either `/* All wellformedness checks were successful. */` or a
11	WARNING section listing check failures grouped under underlined topic
12	headers. Your job: given a parsed theory (data model in
13	interface/ast_types.rs), produce byte-identical report text and the
14	structured API in interface/required_api.md.
15	
16	## Method requirements
17	- Derive the set of checks, their trigger conditions, their exact message
18	  strings, their grouping and ORDER purely by experimenting against the
19	  oracle: write minimal .spthy inputs that trip each check one at a time.
20	  37 pre-captured outputs are in oracle/captures/; example inputs (usable as
21	  starting points for mutations) in oracle/examples/. The .spthy language
22	  syntax may be learned from those examples and the oracle's parse errors.
23	- Log every oracle query's purpose in workspace/QUERIES.log (one line each).
24	- Maintain workspace/BEHAVIOR.md: the growing behavioral spec you infer
25	  (check name, trigger, message template, ordering). This document is a
26	  deliverable equal in importance to the code.
27	- Tests: for each check, a fixture .spthy + expected report snippet, asserted
28	  against your implementation AND spot-verified against the oracle.
29	- Dependencies: std only. You may NOT parse .spthy yourself; your crate's
30	  input is the ast_types.rs data model (write test values directly as Rust
31	  constructors; oracle fixtures verify message/ordering behavior).

===== classic/NSLPK3.spthy (first 90 lines) =====
theory NSLPK3
begin

builtins: asymmetric-encryption

/*
   Protocol:    The classic three message version of the
                Needham-Schroeder-Lowe Public Key Protocol
   Modeler:     Simon Meier
   Date:        June 2012
   Source:      Modeled after the description by Paulson in
                Isabelle/HOL/Auth/NS_Public.thy.

   Status:      working

   Note that we are using explicit global constants for discerning the
   different encryption instead of the implicit sources.
 */


// Public key infrastructure
rule Register_pk:
  [ Fr(~ltkA) ]
  -->
  [ !Ltk($A, ~ltkA), !Pk($A, pk(~ltkA)), Out(pk(~ltkA)) ]

rule Reveal_ltk:
  [ !Ltk(A, ltkA) ] --[ RevLtk(A)    ]-> [ Out(ltkA) ]


/* We formalize the following protocol

  protocol NSLPK3 {
    1. I -> R: {'1',ni,I}pk(R)
    2. I <- R: {'2',ni,nr,R}pk(I)
    3. I -> R: {'3',nr}pk(R)
  }
*/

rule I_1:
  let m1 = aenc{'1', ~ni, $I}pkR
  in
    [ Fr(~ni)
    , !Pk($R, pkR)
    ]
  --[ OUT_I_1(m1)
    ]->
    [ Out( m1 )
    , St_I_1($I, $R, ~ni)
    ]

rule R_1:
  let m1 = aenc{'1', ni, I}pk(ltkR)
      m2 = aenc{'2', ni, ~nr, $R}pkI
  in
    [ !Ltk($R, ltkR)
    , In( m1 )
    , !Pk(I, pkI)
    , Fr(~nr)
    ]
  --[ IN_R_1_ni( ni, m1 )
    , OUT_R_1( m2 )
    , Running(I, $R, <'init',ni,~nr>)
    ]->
    [ Out( m2 )
    , St_R_1($R, I, ni, ~nr)
    ]

rule I_2:
  let m2 = aenc{'2', ni, nr, R}pk(ltkI)
      m3 = aenc{'3', nr}pkR
  in
    [ St_I_1(I, R, ni)
    , !Ltk(I, ltkI)
    , In( m2 )
    , !Pk(R, pkR)
    ]
  --[ IN_I_2_nr( nr, m2)
    , Commit (I, R, <'init',ni,nr>)  // need to log identities explicitely to
    , Running(R, I, <'resp',ni,nr>)  // specify that they must not be
                                     // compromised in the property.
    ]->
    [ Out( m3 )
    , Secret(I,R,nr)
    , Secret(I,R,ni)
    ]

rule R_2:
    [ St_R_1(R, I, ni, nr)
    , !Ltk(R, ltkR)

examples/regression/trace/issue532.spthy (read) :: learn syntax + the facts-LHS-not-RHS check example
examples/classic/NSLPK3.spthy (read) :: learn rule/let/lemma .spthy syntax

p01_unbound_rule :: unbound var in rule action/conclusion -> topic "Unbound variables"
p02_arity :: same fact name diff arity -> topic "Fact arity issues"
p03_mult :: same fact linear vs persistent -> topic "Fact multiplicity issues"
p04_fr :: Fr with arity 2 -> PARSE ERROR (not wf)
p05_lemma_free :: free var in lemma -> topic "Formula terms" (`Free y')
p06_lemma_guard :: simple existential lemma -> SUCCESS (baseline)

p07_reserved_K :: K fact in action -> topic "Reserved names" (on the middle)
p08_out_arity :: Out arity 2 -> PARSE ERROR
p09_fun_reserved :: redeclare builtin fst -> PARSE ERROR (conflicting arities)
p10_hide_lemma :: hide_lemma=NoSuchLemma -> SUCCESS (no wf warning)
p11_sort :: same action across fresh/pub rules -> SUCCESS
p12_binary_op :: multiset + in action -> SUCCESS

p13_reserved_prem :: KU in prem / KD in concl -> "Reserved names" left-hand-side & right-hand-side variants
p14_fr_pub :: Fr($x) -> topic "Fr facts must only use a fresh- or a msg-variable"
p15_inout :: Out in prem, In in concl -> topic "Special facts"
p16_mset :: '+' without multiset builtin -> PARSE ERROR
p17_dup_rule :: duplicate rule name -> PARSE ERROR
p18_dup_lemma :: duplicate lemma name -> PARSE ERROR

p19_lemma_action_missing :: lemma action absent from all rules -> SUCCESS (no check)
p20_inconsistent_sort :: ~x and $x in one rule -> topic "Variable with mismatching sorts or capitalization"
p21_temporal_term :: unguarded temporal var -> topic " Formula guardedness" (leading space)
p22_diff :: diff() without diff flag -> PARSE ERROR
p23_pubconst :: many public constants -> SUCCESS
p24_lemma_arity :: action arity mismatch rule vs lemma -> "Fact arity issues"; lemma fact rendered as raw Haskell Fact{...}

p25_restr_unbound :: free var in restriction -> "Formula terms" (Restriction `..' uses...)
p26_reuse_nonexist :: lemma [reuse] -> SUCCESS
p27_bnames :: builtins hashing/signing/senc -> SUCCESS
p28_process :: simple process -> SUCCESS
p29_pub :: public consts single-use -> SUCCESS
p30_node_in_fact :: nested temporal ordering lemma -> SUCCESS

p31_pubfun :: nullary fn same name as pub const -> SUCCESS
p32_simpub :: 'Alice' & 'alice' same rule -> topic "Public constants with mismatching capitalization"
p33_eq :: subterm-convergent equation -> SUCCESS
p34_dh :: DH exp basic -> SUCCESS
p35_macro :: macros: syntax guess -> PARSE ERROR
p36_pred :: predicates block -> SUCCESS

p37_proc_unbound :: process out(x) unbound -> FATAL "Process not well-formed" (not a wf warning)
p38_pub2 :: 'Server'/'server' across two rules -> public-names cross-rule grouping format
p39_lemma_badsort :: temporal var dropped '#' -> "Formula terms" (Free #i) + guardedness
p40_embedded :: restriction Eq used -> SUCCESS
p41_exists :: use_induction attr -> SUCCESS
p42_bad_builtin :: unknown builtin -> PARSE ERROR (lists valid builtins)

ks1 :: kitchen-sink -> establish canonical topic ORDER (unbound, public-cap, mismatch-sorts, reserved, fr, special, arity, multiplicity, facts-lhs-rhs, formula-terms, guardedness)
p32/ks1 (cat -A) :: exact byte formatting incl trailing spaces, blank-line joins, /* */ wrapper

f_arity3 :: multi-fact multi-item arity format + alpha sort of fact names (bar<foo)
f_pub3 :: 3 public names one rule -> ASCII-sorted 'NODE','Node','node'
f_nullary :: nullary reserved fact -> "K( )"
f_fr :: multiple Fr misuse across/within rules -> separator format

f_subterm :: non-subterm equation -> topic "Subterm Convergence Warning"; placement after Formula terms
f_reducible :: reducible fn in lemma -> "Formula terms" next-line + de Bruijn "Bound N" term rendering
f_subterm_ok :: subterm-convergent eq -> SUCCESS

t_terms :: echo many term shapes via K() -> term pretty printer: x.1, ~x, $p, <..>, h(y), senc(y,z), y^z, (y*z), (y++z), 'const'; fsep wrap
t_xor :: xor -> (a(+)b) unicode, AC-sorted; nullary 'zero'
t_nat :: %+ / %1 -> topic "Nat Sorts"; nat term rendering (y%+z)
t_dh / t_dh2 :: DH exponent shapes -> output truncated / edge; deferred
ks3 :: full ordering probe -> confirm Nat Sorts before Subterm Convergence, both after guardedness

# Behavioral spec inferred from the oracle (wellformedness checker)

All facts below are derived from oracle observation (probes in
scratchpad/probes, captures in oracle/captures). Nothing here is taken from
tamarin-prover source.

## Overall report shape

The oracle emits the wellformedness result inside the pretty-printed theory,
wrapped in a block comment.

Success (empty report):

    /* All wellformedness checks were successful. */

Failure (>=1 topic):

    /*
    WARNING: the following wellformedness checks failed!

    <block1>

    <block2>
    ...
    */

Inner text (what `render_report` produces for a non-empty report) is:

    "WARNING: the following wellformedness checks failed!" + "\n\n" + join(blocks, "\n\n")

Each `block` = topic header + "\n" + underline + "\n\n" + body, where
`underline` = '=' repeated `char_count(topic)` times (topic length INCLUDING
any leading/trailing space). Bodies may themselves contain blank lines.

`render_report` for the empty report returns the success line
`/* All wellformedness checks were successful. */`.
`underline_topic(t)` returns `t + "\n" + "=".repeat(char_count(t))`.

## Canonical topic order (report order)

Observed by kitchen-sink probes ks1 / ks3:

 1. `Unbound variables`
 2. `Public constants with mismatching capitalization`   (public-names report)
 3. `Variable with mismatching sorts or capitalization`
 4. `Reserved names`
 5. `Fr facts must only use a fresh- or a msg-variable`
 6. `Special facts`
 7. `Fact arity issues`
 8. `Fact multiplicity issues`
 9. `Facts occur in the left-hand-side but not in any right-hand-side ` (TRAILING space)
10. `Formula terms`
11. ` Formula guardedness`   (LEADING space)
12. `Nat Sorts`
13. `Subterm Convergence Warning`
14. `Message Derivation Checks`  (appended by the binary; needs Maude - OUT OF SCOPE for check_theory)
    / `Derivation Checks` (timeout variant - OUT OF SCOPE)

Public names (#2) is generated separately and *inserted before* the first of a
set of anchor topics = every topic from #3 onward. `after_public_names_topics()`
returns that anchor list; `insert_wf_before(report, errs, anchors)` inserts
`errs` immediately before the first report entry whose topic is in `anchors`
(appends at end if none present). Unbound variables (#1) is emitted before the
public-names insertion point.

## Per-check triggers and message templates

### Unbound variables
Trigger: a rule uses a variable in its actions/conclusions/embedded that is not
bound by a premise (protocol rules). Body = entries joined by "\n  \n":
    "  rule `NAME' has unbound variables: \n    v1, v2"
(note trailing space after "variables:"). Vars are sorted, comma+space joined.
Var render uses the term pretty-printer (e.g. `x.1`).

### Public constants with mismatching capitalization  (public_names_report)
Trigger: two public constants used in the theory whose names are equal under
ASCII-lowercasing but differ in actual capitalization (e.g. 'Alice','alice').
Body:
    "Identifiers are case-sensitive, mismatched capitalizations are considered as different, i.e., 'ID' is different from 'id'. Check the capitalization of your identifiers.\n\n" + numbered items
Each group of names sharing a lowercased key -> one numbered item:
    "  1. " + occurrences
occurrences group by location; same location: `rule "R1":  name 'Alice', 'alice'`;
different locations joined by ", ": `rule "R1":  name 'Server', rule "R2":  name 'server'`.
Names within a location sorted ASCII ascending. `public_names_report_from_pairs`
takes the raw (location, name) pairs.

### Variable with mismatching sorts or capitalization
Trigger: within one rule, two variables share a base name but differ in sort
prefix (`$x`,`x`,`~x`) or capitalization.
Body header (fixed):
    "Possible reasons:\n1. Identifiers are case sensitive, i.e.,'x' and 'X' are considered to be different.\n2. The same holds for sorts:, i.e., '$x', 'x', and '~x' are considered to be different.\n"
then per rule:
    "  rule `A5': \n    1. $s, ~s"
(trailing space after rule name; numbered groups, each a comma-joined variant list).

### Reserved names
Trigger: a rule uses a fact whose name is reserved (K, KU, KD, and others) in a
position. Per (rule, position) entry, joined by "\n  \n":
    "  Rule `R1' contains facts with reserved names on left-hand-side:\n    <facts>"
Position phrases (note asymmetry): LHS `on left-hand-side:`, actions
`on the middle:`, RHS `on the right-hand-side:`. Facts rendered by the fact
pretty-printer, comma-joined via fsep (wraps ~col 80, continuation indent 4).

### Fr facts must only use a fresh- or a msg-variable
Trigger: an `Fr(t)` fact whose argument is not a fresh- or msg-variable
(e.g. `Fr($x)`). Entries joined by "\n  \n":
    "  rule `R1' fact: Fr( $p )"

### Special facts
Trigger: a reserved I/O fact used in a disallowed position (`Out`/`Fr`/`K` in
premises, `In`/`K` in conclusions). Per (rule, side) entry joined "\n  \n":
    "  rule `R1' uses disallowed facts on left-hand-side:\n    Out( x )"
(sides: `on left-hand-side:` / `on right-hand-side:`; lowercase "rule").

### Fact arity issues
Trigger: same fact name used with >1 arity anywhere (rules and lemma facts).
Body:
    "Same fact is used with different arities, i.e., Fact('A','B') is different from Fact('A'). \nCheck the arguments of your facts.\n  " + factBlocks
Conflicting facts sorted by lowercased name. factBlock (each prefixed "\n"):
    "\n  Fact `foo':\n\n" + items + "\n  "
item i: "    {i}. Rule `R', arity {n}\n         {factPP}" ; items joined by "\n    \n".
Facts from a rule render via the fact pretty-printer; facts from a lemma render
as the raw Haskell `Fact {factTag = ProtoFact Linear "A" 2, factAnnotations = fromList [], factTerms = [Bound 2,Bound 1]}` (de Bruijn) -- see gaps.

### Fact multiplicity issues
Same structure as arity but keyed on multiplicity; header:
    "Same fact is used with different multiplicities, i.e., !Fact() (Persistent fact) exists along with Fact() (Linear) in your rules. \nCheck the multiplicity (persistence) of your facts.\n  " + factBlocks
item i: "    {i}. Rule `R', multiplicity (persistence) {Linear|Persistent}\n         {factPP}".

### Facts occur in the left-hand-side but not in any right-hand-side  (fact_lhs_occur_no_rhs)
Trigger: a user (non-special) fact identity (name,arity,multiplicity) that
appears in some premise but never in any conclusion. Numbered items joined
"\n  \n":
    "  1. in rule \"r1\":  factName `LongFactNameExt' arity: 0 multiplicity: Linear" +
      optional " . Perhaps you want to use the fact in rule \"r1\":  factName `LongFactNameExact' arity: 0 multiplicity: Linear"
The suggestion is the nearest RHS fact by edit distance (below a threshold);
omitted when nothing is close enough (rule r4 / NotInAnyRHS). Special facts
(Fr,In,Out,K,...) are excluded from both sides.

### Formula terms
Trigger: a lemma/restriction formula uses a term that is not a public constant
or a bound node/message variable (free variable, or reducible function symbol).
Body:
    "  Lemma `L1' uses terms of the wrong form: `Free y'\n  \n" + fixed_help
When the offending term list is short it stays inline after the colon; when
longer/complex it breaks to an indented next line
`    `t1', `t2'`. Reducible/complex terms render with de Bruijn `Bound N` (gap).
fixed_help =
    "  The only allowed terms are public constants and bound node and\n  message variables. If you encounter free message variables, then\n  you might have forgotten a #-prefix. Sort prefixes can only be\n  dropped where this is unambiguous. Moreover, reducible function\n  symbols are disallowed."
Entity label is `Lemma` or `Restriction`.

### Formula guardedness   (topic has a LEADING space)
Trigger: a lemma formula cannot be converted to a guarded formula (e.g.
unguarded quantified variable). Body:
    "  Lemma `L2' cannot be converted to a guarded formula:\n    unguarded variable(s) '#j' in the subformula\n      \"<pp>\"\n    in the formula\n      \"<pp>\""
`<pp>` is the unicode formula pretty-print. (Full unicode formula printer + the
guardedness algorithm are a partial area -- see gaps.)

### Nat Sorts
Trigger: a variable used in a `%+` (nat) context that is not of sort nat.
Entries joined "\n  \n":
    "  y in term (y%+z) must be of sort nat"
(needs nat-sort inference -- structural only, see gaps).

### Subterm Convergence Warning
Trigger: a user equation `l = r` that is not subterm convergent. Body:
    "  User-defined equations must be convergent and have the finite variant property. The following equations are not subterm convergent. If you are sure that the set of equations is nevertheless convergent and has the finite variant property, you can ignore this warning and continue \n\n" + equations + "\n   \n For more information, please refer to the manual : https://tamarin-prover.com/manual/master/book/010_modeling-issues.html "
each equation rendered "    {lhsPP} = {rhsPP}" (needs subterm-convergence
decision + term printer -- partial, see gaps).

## Term pretty-printer (observed)
- Var: sort prefix + base name + ("." + idx if idx>0). prefixes: Fresh `~`,
  Pub `$`, Nat `%`, Node `#`, Msg/Untagged ``.
- Pair / App("pair",[a,b]): `<a, b, ...>` (right-nested pairs flattened).
- App(f, args): `f(a, b)` ("(" + " " ... " )" via <+>; nullary -> `f`).
- PubLit s: `'s'`.
- BinOp: Exp `a^b` (no outer parens); Mult `(a*b)`; Union `(a++b)`;
  Xor `(a⊕b)` U+2295, AC-sorted; NatPlus `(a%+b)`.
- NatOne -> `%1`.
- Fact render: [`!` if persistent] + name + "(" + (" " + args.join(", ") + " )"
  if args else " )").

*/` on failure. Each block = topic header + a `=`-underline exactly as long as the topic (counting significant leading/trailing spaces) + blank line + body; blocks are joined by blank lines. I enumerated 13 distinct warning topics in a fixed canonical order: (1) Unbound variables, (2) Public constants with mismatching capitalization, (3) Variable with mismatching sorts or capitalization, (4) Reserved names, (5) Fr facts must only use a fresh- or a msg-variable, (6) Special facts, (7) Fact arity issues, (8) Fact multiplicity issues, (9) Facts occur in the left-hand-side but not in any right-hand-side (trailing space), (10) Formula terms, (11) Formula guardedness (leading space), (12) Nat Sorts, (13) Subterm Convergence Warning — plus (14) Message Derivation Checks / Derivation Checks which are appended by the binary via Maude and are not AST-derivable. The public-names report (#2) is generated separately and inserted immediately before the first present topic in an anchor list (#3..#13); `after_public_names_topics()`/`insert_wf_before` expose that mechanism. I reverse-engineered exact byte templates for every topic (trailing-space quirks, `
  
` 2-space separators, the nested `  Fact `name':` arity/multiplicity layout, the `factName `X' arity: N multiplicity: Linear` LHS/RHS format with a nearest-neighbour "Perhaps you want" suggestion gated at edit-distance ≤ 3), the term pretty-printer (`~x`/`$p`/`x.1`, `<a, b>` pairs, `a^b`, `(a*b)`, `(a++b)`, `(a⊕b)`, `(a%+b)`, `Name( args )` facts), and a single-line unicode formula printer (∀ ∃ ⇒ ∧ ∨ ¬ ⊤ ⊥ @). Key position-dependent finding: reserved facts are {K,KU,KD} on LHS/RHS but {K,KU,KD,In,Out,Fr} in actions; In/Out/Fr misplaced on LHS/RHS are "Special facts" instead. Unguarded restrictions and SAPIC unbound process vars are fatal errors, not warnings.</behavior_spec_summary>

Formula guardedness uses a single-line formula printer; formulas the oracle wraps across multiple indented lines will not be byte-identical. Subterm Convergence: uses the structural 'rhs is a subterm of lhs' test and does not model the finite-variant property; multi-line wrapped equation rendering (eccDAA-style deeply nested terms) is not reproduced; a `convergent` flag suppresses the check.
Nat Sorts fires on any non-Nat-sorted variable inside a %+ term and relies on the parser having assigned sorts; full nat sort inference is not modeled.
AC operator argument ordering: xor operands are string-sorted (matching probe t_xor) but multiset/mult/nat operands keep source order; exact parity needs tamarin's term ordering.

fsep line-wrapping of fact lists in Reserved names / Special facts (observed wrapping at ~column 80 in probe t_terms) is not implemented; only single-line comma joins, so very wide fact lists will not match.

public_names_report collects public constants from rule facts only (not from lemma/restriction formulas); the multi-conflict-group item separator and group ordering were inferred from single-group observations.

check_if_lemmas_in_theory: the missing-lemma set-difference logic is implemented and tested, but the exact oracle render string could not be observed through the file-only oracle (no CLI --prove path), so its message text is an unverified placeholder.
